metadata.title: Johnson Stores IC Diligence Deck
metadata.author: KPMG LLP
metadata.subject: Investment committee diligence review with benchmark overlay

slides.title: Johnson Stores IC Diligence Review
slides.subtitle: Extensive investment committee pack covering commercial, QoE, cash, operations, and Starbucks benchmark context

slides.title: Contents

sections.title: Executive summary
items: Growth, quality, and risk headline evidence

sections.title: Benchmark and valuation lens
items: IC bid guardrails and scenario ranges

slides.title: IC readout: growth is strong, quality is mixed
slides.strapline: Johnson Stores has scaled rapidly with strong reported margins, but the committee should anchor valuation to normalized cash earnings and explicit downside scenarios.

columns.heading: Scale
body: Revenue increased from $309.9M in 2023 to $518.5M in 2025, a 29.4% CAGR over two years.
The business now operates 91 stores across a broad multi-state footprint with 848k square feet of capacity.
Operating income rose from $58.6M to $98.0M while maintaining approximately 19% operating margin.
Implication: commercial demand and footprint expansion have translated into meaningful scale.

columns.heading: Quality
body: All 19 of 19 core verification checks passed, including financial statement tie-outs and ledger consistency tests.
Gross margin held at 53.9% in each year, indicating stable merchandise economics at reported levels.
Cash conversion improved materially from negative in 2023 to 80% of net income in 2025.
Implication: core accounting reliability is high, but earnings quality still requires normalization discipline.

columns.heading: Risk
body: Verification flags ten QoE issues, including one-time events, churn exposure, and owner-related adjustments requiring evidence.
Known customer churn risk is quantified at $19.6M aggregate impact, concentrated in 2022 to 2024 losses.
Working capital intensity remains elevated, with operating NWC reaching $118.2M and 138.4% of monthly revenue at end-2025.
Implication: underwriting should separate proven base economics from unresolved downside drivers.

columns.heading: Decision
body: Anchor value on normalized EBITDA and stress tested cash generation rather than headline operating profit growth.
Use scenario pricing bands tied to churn realization and timing of policy-related QoE adjustments.
Require pre-close diligence closes on reserves, related-party normalization, and retention plan execution.
Implication: proceed with guarded bid structure, not unconditional multiple expansion.

slides.title: IC ask and recommended transaction posture
slides.strapline: The transaction is investable with guardrails: proceed on proven operating momentum, but hard-wire downside protection around churn and adjustment uncertainty.
body: Johnson has demonstrated material scale-up, with revenue up 67% from 2023 to 2025 and operating income growth of nearly $39.4M across the same period.
Data-room integrity is strong: trial balance, cash flow, payroll, and aging schedules reconcile, allowing confidence in baseline arithmetic and trend direction.
Ten QoE items remain open, including inventory reserve policy, gift-card breakage timing, lease adjustments, and owner add-backs requiring corroboration.
Customer churn risk of $19.6M is not embedded in base reported performance and should be modeled as explicit downside sensitivity instead of normalized fact.
Advance with a structured bid anchored to normalized earnings, include downside-linked protections, and condition final pricing on closure of evidence gaps.

slides.title: Revenue scaled 67% across 2023 to 2025
slides.strapline: The top-line trajectory supports a growth thesis, but the committee should test how much of the step-up is recurring versus event-driven.
body: Revenue increased from $309.9M in 2023 to $384.8M in 2024 and $518.5M in 2025, indicating sustained demand and operating throughput expansion.
The two-year CAGR of 29.4% materially exceeds typical mature retail growth bands and therefore warrants quality-of-growth decomposition.
Event impacts include both recurring and non-recurring components, so headline growth should not be fully capitalized without normalization.
Use growth strength as strategic support, while tying valuation multiple confidence to recurring revenue persistence evidence.

slides.title: Margin profile is stable with cash rebound
slides.strapline: Reported operating margin stayed near 19%, and cash conversion improved after a working-capital-heavy 2023 period.
body: Operating margin held at 18.9% in 2023, 19.8% in 2024, and 18.9% in 2025 while gross margin remained fixed at 53.9% each year.
Cash from operations moved from negative $10.7M in 2023 to positive $58.7M in 2024 and $78.6M in 2025 as working-capital drag partially normalized.
CFO to net income reached 80.2% in 2025, signaling improved conversion but still leaving room to close toward best-in-class conversion levels.
Treat 2024 to 2025 cash profile as improved baseline, but preserve downside reserves for renewed NWC build pressure.

slides.title: Business model supports breadth and density
slides.strapline: Johnson combines broad physical coverage with electronics specialization, creating scale opportunities with execution risk in consistency and inventory turns.
body: The company runs 91 electronics-focused stores totaling approximately 848k square feet, with average store size of roughly 9.3k square feet.
Fifty-one stores opened in 2019 or later, indicating rapid recent expansion that increases execution complexity in staffing, assortment, and working capital.
Revenue is recognized at point of sale with policy overlays for returns, gift cards, and loyalty redemptions, making policy precision critical to comparability.
Customer event history shows meaningful new-customer additions but only partial recurring quality, with 55.3% of wins tagged as recurring.
Growth thesis is credible, but diligence should test repeatability of post-opening productivity and recurring customer retention quality.

slides.title: Store footprint indicates scaled execution scope
slides.strapline: Recent expansion underpins growth but also increases standardization demands across managers, inventory controls, and localized demand forecasting.
insights: The pace of openings implies that process maturity can lag physical growth, especially for inventory planning and staffing consistency.
IC diligence should require store-cohort productivity analysis to confirm later cohorts are converging toward mature-store margins.

slides.title: Customer events drive significant volume swings
slides.strapline: Event-driven inflows are meaningful and include both recurring and non-recurring components, shaping the quality-of-growth debate.
body: Event ledger records 47 customer wins versus 4 losses, with gross win impact of $571.7M and loss impact of $19.6M over the dataset history.
Running recurring monthly revenue additions increased from $2.7M in 2023 to $9.2M in 2025, indicating improved retention of prior wins.
The four losses remain economically material and are concentrated in years that influence current underwriting assumptions.
Use a recurring-quality filter in valuation to avoid paying full value for episodic event uplifts.

slides.title: Churn risk is manageable but valuation-relevant
slides.strapline: Churn is not thesis-breaking in isolation, but it is large enough to shift EBITDA and leverage outcomes under downside cases.
body: Verification flags four lost customers with total impact of $19.6M, including concentration in 2022 and 2024 that can carry into forward-year base setting.
Loss reasons include price sensitivity and customer budget pressure, indicating both competitive and macro sensitivity channels.
Given the quantified scale, churn should be modeled as explicit scenario stress rather than absorbed implicitly into growth assumptions.
Retention mitigation plans, pricing discipline, and account-level margin contribution data are necessary before tightening downside bands.
Preserve downside valuation protection until top-customer renewal visibility is fully documented.

slides.title: Event detail explains growth quality variance
slides.strapline: The same headline growth period contains both durable recurring additions and one-time pulses; the committee should value them differently.
slides.heading: Event quality split

body.text: What the chart and table show

body: One-time revenue varied materially by year, with peaks in 2023 and 2025 that can inflate period-on-period comparisons if not normalized.
Recurring run-rate additions stepped up each year, suggesting that the customer engine improved even as one-time noise remained present.

body.text: What this means for IC valuation

body: Base-case growth assumptions should rely more heavily on recurring run-rate trends than total event-adjusted reported growth.
Bid structure can share upside if one-time gains convert to recurring outcomes but should not prepay that conversion today.
slides.noteSource: Source: event_impacts.xlsx; annualized from monthly event schedules

slides.title: Commercial upside needs disciplined conversion
slides.strapline: The win engine is active, but value creation depends on converting first-order events into durable recurring revenue and resilient account margins.
body: Recurring win quality appears to be improving based on run-rate build, creating a plausible pathway to sustain high-teens to low-twenties growth trajectories.
Store density and expanded footprint provide cross-sell and omnichannel opportunities if inventory and pricing discipline are maintained.
Rapid expansion and mixed event quality increase the risk that reported growth outpaces process maturity in retention, forecasting, and margin governance.
Without account-level profitability governance, high-volume growth can still dilute cash conversion and working-capital efficiency.
Tie upside valuation credit to measurable recurring conversion KPIs, not just gross booking momentum.

slides.title: Financial scale expanded materially in 3 years
slides.strapline: Revenue and operating income both increased significantly, but valuation quality depends on isolating durable earnings from one-time effects.
body: Revenue rose from $309.9M to $518.5M while operating income increased from $58.6M to $98.0M between 2023 and 2025.
Gross profit grew by $112.5M over the period, reflecting both volume growth and stable gross margin mechanics.
Strong reported expansion can mask timing effects; therefore, bridge analysis should isolate recurring run-rate and non-recurring events.
The IC can underwrite momentum, but only after normalization of event-linked components and downside sensitivity calibration.

slides.title: Reported margin resilience supports base case
slides.strapline: Reported gross and operating margins remained stable through expansion, improving confidence in baseline operating model coherence.
body: Gross margin remained at 53.9% across all three years, while operating margin stayed in a tight 18.9% to 19.8% band.
Net income tracked operating income closely in the dataset, simplifying reported trend interpretation for IC context.
Margin stability does not eliminate QoE risk because policy-sensitive items and event timing can preserve headline margin while altering cash quality.
Use margin stability as baseline support, but keep normalization and policy diligence in the core decision package.

slides.title: EBITDA bridge: reported to normalized (1/2)
slides.strapline: Normalization begins with reported EBITDA and removes event-driven components that should not be annualized in the base valuation case.
slides.heading: Bridge mechanics by year
body: Reported EBITDA is proxied as operating income plus depreciation and then adjusted for one-time revenue and known non-recurring items.
One-time revenue is removed from base earnings because repeatability is not yet evidenced at portfolio-level confidence.
The normalization delta is meaningful enough to influence leverage sizing and valuation confidence bands.
IC discussion should focus on which adjustments migrate to base versus remain in sensitivity scenarios.
slides.noteSource: Source: income_statement.xlsx, fixed_assets.xlsx, event_impacts.xlsx; values in $M

slides.title: EBITDA bridge: risk overlays and scenarios (2/2)
slides.strapline: After base normalization, the key valuation swing factor is churn realization plus unresolved policy adjustments that remain outside booked base.
slides.heading: Risk overlay bridge
body: Insurance recovery (+$3.5M) and restructuring costs (-$6.2M) are identified one-time items that require consistent classification and support.
Customer churn risk of $19.6M remains outside booked normalization and is modeled as scenario exposure until retention evidence closes.
Partial churn realization compresses normalized EBITDA materially and can tighten debt sizing and valuation tolerance.
IC should gate final pricing on closure of these overlay lines and documented policy treatment.

slides.title: QoE issue register is broad but actionable
slides.strapline: The issue set is manageable with targeted diligence, but unresolved items are sufficiently material to require structured pre-close closure criteria.
insights: Most open items are not arithmetic failures; they are classification and evidence-quality decisions that directly affect valuation confidence.
A tightly sequenced diligence sprint can convert this register from uncertainty to bounded decision inputs before final bid.

slides.title: Downside scenarios are the key IC swing factor
slides.strapline: Even with strong reported momentum, downside realization on churn and policy adjustments can materially alter return profile and leverage safety.
body: Base case assumes normalized 2025 EBITDA of $96.7M after one-time revenue removal and no additional policy adjustments.
Downside cases apply incremental churn realization and partial policy reserve actions, reducing EBITDA into the high-70s to high-80s range.
Scenario spread is wide enough to justify bid guardrails, earn-out mechanics, or price-step structures linked to post-close retention outcomes.
Do not collapse scenario outcomes into a single point estimate until customer and policy evidence thresholds are met.

chart.source: Source: derived from verification_report.json and bridge assumptions

slides.title: NWC build warrants purchase price discipline
slides.strapline: Operating NWC expanded significantly through growth, which can suppress free cash conversion if not actively managed post-close.
insights: Absolute operating NWC more than doubled to $118.2M by end-2025, creating funding intensity despite improving percentage ratios.
Closing mechanics should include explicit NWC targets and leakage protection to avoid value transfer via working-capital drift.

slides.title: Aging profile is healthy with limited tail risk
slides.strapline: AR and AP concentration in current and 1-30 day buckets indicates generally disciplined collections and payables cadence at snapshot date.
body: 91.0% of receivables sit in Current or 1-30 day buckets, with no exposure in over-90-day balances at the measured point.
94.0% of payables are also within Current or 1-30 days, suggesting vendor obligations are largely managed within agreed terms.
Healthy aging does not remove risk from rapid scale; monitoring of collections discipline by cohort remains important.
Use aging quality as supportive evidence, but keep working-capital governance in the first 100-day value creation plan.

slides.title: Cash generation improved after 2023 drag
slides.strapline: Operating cash has recovered strongly, but sustained conversion depends on preventing renewed receivable and inventory expansion.
body: Cash from operations moved from negative $10.7M in 2023 to $58.7M in 2024 and $78.6M in 2025 as growth absorption normalized.
Capex remained relatively stable around $0.9M annually in 2024 and 2025, limiting incremental investment drag on operating cash.
Ending cash reached $98.8M in 2025, supporting liquidity but not eliminating the need for disciplined NWC management.
IC should underwrite improving cash resilience with explicit downside thresholds tied to NWC behavior.

slides.title: Day-1 cash conversion priorities are clear
slides.strapline: The first operating quarter post-close should focus on receivables velocity, inventory turns, and cohort-level store productivity governance.
body: Institute weekly collections governance on top customer accounts and monitor AR days by cohort to prevent expansion-led working-capital leakage.
Standardize replenishment parameters and slow-mover reserve governance to reduce cash trapped in inventory and avoid margin-dilutive markdown cycles.
Track new-store ramp curves against mature benchmarks to detect subscale cohorts early and trigger pricing, assortment, or labor corrective action.
Assign a joint CFO-COO weekly cash council with quantified targets for CFO conversion and operating NWC release through day 100.

slides.title: Workforce scale supports current footprint
slides.strapline: Headcount composition aligns with a store-led model, with concentration in operations and logistics and a smaller digital and analytics layer.
insights: Operational headcount concentration is appropriate for a physical retail model, but digital and analytics capacity remains proportionally modest.
Talent strategy should prioritize merchandising analytics and e-commerce productivity to sustain growth without proportionate working-capital expansion.

slides.title: Leadership bench is credible but stretched
slides.strapline: The core leadership team has relevant tenure and operating experience, yet growth velocity suggests a need for additional execution bandwidth.
body: The CEO has 14 years at the company and the CFO and COO each bring seven years of internal context, supporting continuity in strategic execution.
Current management has overseen significant footprint and revenue expansion, indicating ability to execute growth-oriented plans.
Rapid expansion to 91 stores and elevated working-capital demands increase management load across forecasting, controls, and integration of new cohorts.
The VP Sales tenure of two years is relatively recent; institutionalized retention and pricing governance should reduce key-person dependency.
Post-close governance should add functional depth in finance operations and commercial analytics to de-risk scale execution.

slides.title: Supplier and inventory factors need monitoring
slides.strapline: Vendor terms are generally balanced, but lead-time variability and inventory intensity can amplify cash and service-level volatility.
slides.heading: Supply chain risk signals
body: The supplier base includes 30 vendors with average lead time of 32.7 days and a maximum observed lead time of 57 days.
Payment terms are mixed across Net 30, Net 45, and Net 60, creating manageable but non-trivial payables timing variability.
Longer lead-time tails combined with rapid store scaling can force higher safety stock and raise inventory carrying costs.
Supplier concentration and term discipline should be linked to margin protection and NWC release objectives.

slides.title: Operational plan should prioritize control depth
slides.strapline: The value-creation plan should emphasize scalable controls and decision rights so growth does not outpace financial quality and service reliability.
body: Build standardized monthly control packs covering inventory reserves, markdown governance, receivables quality, and store-level contribution margins.
Implement account-level renewal and pricing governance to reduce churn surprises and improve recurring revenue conversion confidence.
Establish clear owner accountability across CFO, COO, and commercial leads with quantified KPI thresholds and escalation triggers.
A control-first integration path should improve earnings quality consistency and narrow valuation uncertainty during the first ownership year.

slides.title: Johnson growth outpaced SBUX trajectory
slides.strapline: Johnson is much smaller in absolute scale, but its recent growth rate materially exceeds the large-cap benchmark trajectory.
body: Johnson revenue grew from $309.9M to $518.5M between 2023 and 2025, while SBUX revenue moved from $29.1B to $36.0B over the same fiscal years.
Johnson's two-year CAGR of 29.4% far exceeds SBUX's growth profile, reflecting earlier-stage scaling dynamics rather than mature-network behavior.
Higher growth does not imply lower risk; it often coincides with higher working-capital and process-maturity pressures.
Benchmark should inform direction and ambition, while valuation should remain anchored to Johnson-specific risk-adjusted cash quality.

slides.title: Scale gap frames realistic margin ambition
slides.strapline: SBUX provides a benchmark on operating discipline, but Johnson's current size suggests a different path and risk envelope to reach similar resilience.
body: In 2025, SBUX revenue is approximately 69x Johnson revenue, highlighting fundamentally different operating leverage and procurement bargaining positions.
Johnson revenue equals roughly 1.44% of SBUX revenue, indicating that benchmark application must be directional rather than directly transposed.
Johnson operating margin around 19% appears strong relative to SBUX 2023-2025 range of 14.3% to 16.8%, but quality-of-earnings proof still matters.
Set IC targets on durable cash conversion and retention quality before rewarding headline margin premium in final multiple selection.

slides.title: Benchmark-informed IC valuation scenarios
slides.strapline: Scenario ranges combine Johnson normalized earnings with benchmark-informed discipline on growth quality, cash conversion, and downside tolerance.
slides.heading: Decision bands for pricing
body: Base case uses normalized EBITDA of $96.7M with continued margin stability and improving cash conversion discipline.
Downside case incorporates partial churn realization and selected policy adjustments, reducing EBITDA into the high-$80M range.
Severe case applies deeper churn and working-capital stress, lowering EBITDA near $77M and pressuring return thresholds.
Bid structure should link price realization to evidence closure and realized retention quality.

chart.source: Source: bridge assumptions and benchmark context

slides.noteSource: Source: verification_report.json, annual_metrics.csv, internal scenario framework

slides.title: IC decision framework and bid guardrails
slides.strapline: The committee should convert diligence uncertainty into explicit pricing and term mechanics rather than absorbing it into narrative confidence.
body: Confirm customer retention evidence for top exposure accounts and secure documented treatment for policy-sensitive QoE items before final commitment.
Anchor headline valuation to normalized earnings, apply downside scenario discounting, and reserve upside participation for verified recurring conversion.
Incorporate working-capital true-up discipline, targeted indemnities, and potential contingent value features linked to retention outcomes.
Advance only when downside tolerance, evidence quality, and return thresholds align under the same scenario set.

slides.title: Source index and coverage by workstream
slides.strapline: Primary claims in this deck map directly to provided data-room and SEC benchmark files to preserve evidentiary traceability for IC review.

rows: Store scale, opening pace, event history

insights: All quantitative claims in the core deck are tied to explicit source files, supporting fast verification during committee challenge sessions.
Where values are scenario-derived, the deck labels them as modeled assumptions rather than historical facts.
slides.notes: Source: data room manifest and SBUX annual metrics extract

slides.title: QoE issues and diligence closure status
slides.strapline: Issue tracking converts uncertainty into executable diligence actions, helping the committee separate decision-critical open items from monitorable residual risk.
insights: The issue list is complete enough to support scenario-based decisioning now, but not yet complete enough for unconditional pricing confidence.
Closing the top quantified items and at least two major unquantified policy areas should materially narrow valuation uncertainty.

slides.disclaimer: This material was prepared for discussion purposes and reflects analysis of the provided synthetic diligence data room and benchmark extracts. It does not constitute an audit opinion or investment advice. Distribution is restricted to authorized transaction stakeholders.
